chapters.title: The stories we tell about the world are often wrong
chapters.text:
In 2017, researchers asked a sample of Swedes to take a quiz about global issues. The answers were revealing. Sixty-one percent guessed that only one in five girls in low-income countries went to primary school. Thirty-seven percent thought extreme poverty had doubled over the past twenty years, while another twenty-seven percent thought it had stayed the same. Seventy-seven percent believed average life expectancy around the world was under sixty-one years.

Putting those answers together, you get a picture of how the average Swede sees the planet. Poverty is everywhere, gender equality is a distant dream, and long life is still a perk reserved for rich countries. In short, the world is not getting better, and sixty-six percent of Swedes believe it is actually getting worse. They are not alone. YouGov polling found that sixty-five percent of Americans, seventy percent of Australians, and eighty-one percent of French respondents agree.

That gloomy starting point shapes a lot of other beliefs. Maybe fighting poverty is hopeless, and we should stop pretending. Maybe we need radical new measures because the old ones have failed. Those questions feel reasonable, but only if the starting point is right. What if the world is not getting worse?

In fact, it is getting better. In 1966, half the world lived in extreme poverty. Today the number is closer to nine percent. Sixty years was the typical life span back then, and now it is seventy-two. In low-income countries, sixty percent of girls finish primary school. We have not solved every problem, but we are clearly on the right track.

India alone cut extreme poverty from forty-two percent to twelve percent in the twenty years between 1997 and 2017.

Hans Rosling called real data about the world therapeutic, because it lifts optimism and rebuilds confidence in the future. Are you ready for this dose of therapy? Stick with us as we unpack the instincts that warp your view.

chapters.title: The old split between the West and the rest no longer fits
chapters.text:
The belief that the world is going downhill comes from what Rosling called the negativity instinct, the habit of assuming the worst. A second common myth is the huge gap between so-called developing and developed nations. In 1965, only forty-four countries had high child survival rates and small families, while the other one hundred twenty-five had the opposite. Things look very different today. Most of the world now lives in countries that are developed or on their way there.

Seventy-five percent of the global population has a middle income.

Since the labels developing and developed no longer fit, Rosling broke the world into four income levels. Here is what life looks like on each one.

On the first level, daily income is about one dollar. Families usually have five children who spend the day hauling water and gathering firewood. They have no transport, and they go barefoot. If someone gets sick, the family cannot afford medicine. A single drought can leave them hungry. Two centuries ago, this was the life of eighty-five percent of the world. Today, about one billion people still live this way.

On the second level, daily income is about four dollars. Electricity is patchy, so cooking happens on a gas stove. Children wear sandals, ride bikes to bring water, and walk to school. Roughly three billion people live like this. It is a clear step up, but illness can still wipe out a familys savings.

On the third level, daily income is around sixteen dollars. Cold running water and a fridge are now normal. Children have time to study under steady electricity, and motorcycles let parents commute to a town for work. The family can put money aside for a holiday or for college. About two billion people live like this.

On the fourth level, daily income is around sixty-four dollars. Family members usually have more than ten years of schooling, hot and cold water, and a car. About one billion people enjoy this life.

These four levels are a path most countries travel. Roslings grandmother, born in 1891, lived in a Sweden at level one. When his mother was born, Sweden was at level two. He was born in 1948, when his family had reached level three. His children grew up in a Sweden at level four. The good news is that the climb is faster than it used to be.

chapters.title: Poverty is not a life sentence
chapters.text:
The old West-versus-the-rest split often comes from the generalization instinct. Rosling tried to break that habit in his students by showing them the world up close. He took them on trips where they could see life at each level for themselves. In Uganda, the students were stunned by how organized the city of Kampala really was.

The next step in clearing our view is to avoid jumping to conclusions. In Tunisia, you often see houses with a half-finished second floor. Many travelers assume the families are poor and disorganized. The truth is more interesting. Most people on the second and third income levels cannot get credit, and they rarely have a bank account. Saving cash in a bank does not always pay off, because inflation eats into the value. So families use their spare cash to buy bricks and build a second floor a little at a time, working toward the home they want.

The third step is to change the basis of our categories. We should sort by income, not by country, religion, or culture. Citizens of the same country can live wildly different lives. Tunisia is a good example. You can meet people from all four levels there. Picture their morning routines. Some Tunisians clean their teeth with a stick. Others at the second level share a single plastic toothbrush. At the third level, every family member has a personal toothbrush. Many Tunisians at the fourth level use an electric toothbrush.

People with the same income tend to live in similar ways, no matter their country or religion. The Dollar Street project proves it with photos. Patchwork roofs are common for families at the second level, whether they live in the Philippines, Liberia, or Colombia.

The average Christian family and the average Muslim family typically have three children.

So when someone tells you that a region will never reach the fourth level, be careful. You are probably looking at what Rosling called the destiny instinct. That instinct says some countries are doomed to stay poor because of their geography, history, or values. People often say this about sub-Saharan Africa. Yet half a century ago, China and South Korea were poorer than many of those African nations. Skeptics said they would never escape poverty. Time proved them wrong.

Data also suggests that in forty years, sixty percent of people at the fourth level will live outside the West. The International Monetary Fund backs this up. The fastest growth, above five percent, is happening in countries on the second level, not the fourth.

chapters.title: The world does not move in straight lines
chapters.text:
Straight-line thinking is another instinct that can trap us. A classic example is the question of world population. Many people assume the numbers will keep climbing. The reality is closer to balance. Over the past fifty years, the average family size has dropped from five children to two and a half. United Nations data shows the number has held steady for years, and the global population is expected to stop growing by 2060.

Yes, low-income families still tend to have more children. That is not a cultural preference. The reasons are the need for child labor, low child survival rates, and limited access to contraception. All of those causes come from extreme poverty. Once poverty eases, two children per family becomes the norm around the world. We already see it happening. In the 1970s, the average Bangladeshi family had nine members. Today, parents in Bangladesh typically have two kids. The same shift is underway from Brazil to Indonesia.

Between 1972 and 2017, life expectancy in Bangladesh climbed from fifty-two to seventy-three years.

It also helps to remember that not every chart is a straight line. Take cavities among twelve-year-olds. The numbers stay low at the first income level, then climb through the second and third, and drop again at the fourth.

Sometimes straight-line charts are used to stoke a sense of urgency, another instinct at work. Some of the people who draw them believe urgency is the only way to make others act. The pattern is a little like the shepherd who cried wolf so many times that no one listened when a real wolf showed up. Constant urgency produces stress and apathy. Stress makes us act without thinking, which rarely ends well. Apathy makes us believe that nothing we do will help. So exaggeration makes the problem worse.

Rosling believed we should rewrite the story on climate change. Humanity has already handled big problems like lead in gasoline and chemicals that thinned the ozone layer. Cutting emissions is another challenge we can meet.

A fun fact. Chinas birth rate fell from six children to three about ten years before Maos one-child policy began.

chapters.title: Many viewpoints bring a clearer picture of the world
chapters.text:
Why is diversity such a hot topic these days? It offers a strong defense against the single perspective instinct. People often hunt for one neat cause or one neat answer, but reality is more layered than that.

Even good ideas can fail when they miss key details. Some experts once argued that adding more teachers and more textbooks would lift school results sharply. Those things matter, but giving children time to study matters more. After school, many kids help their families with chores until sunset. Without electric light, they cannot do their homework. So electricity is a quiet but powerful lever on academic results.

Looking at problems from several angles helps every country.

Let us take another example. Many fourth-level countries spend around three thousand six hundred dollars per person on health care, while the United States spends about nine thousand four hundred. You might assume Americans live longer than other wealthy nations. In fact, US life expectancy is shorter by about three years. One of the biggest reasons is the lack of basic public health insurance.

The blame instinct feeds the single perspective. Once we have someone to point at, we stop looking for the deeper causes. Many people remember the tragedy of four thousand refugees who tried to reach Europe by sea in 2015. At first glance, the smugglers who sold them flimsy boats for a thousand euros each were the obvious villains. They did exploit a desperate situation. There were less obvious causes, though. Why did the refugees not simply buy a plane ticket if they had a thousand euros? Rosling checked. Flights were not full, and reaching the airport was not the problem.

The researcher concluded that vague immigration rules were the real culprit. Airlines that fly undocumented migrants to Europe must pay to send them back. That rule does not apply to refugees. In practice, airport staff do not have time to verify every travelers status, especially when some embassies take eight months to decide. A visa is the deciding factor. Without one, a plane ticket gets you nowhere. Ignore that rule, and the problem will not go away.

chapters.title: The picture the media shows is often out of scale
chapters.text:
News tends to focus on events that stir strong feelings, so we see dramatic footage of a bear attack more often than the steady drumbeat of domestic violence. That kind of out-of-scale coverage feeds the fear instinct.

Plane crashes are another telling example. After a 1944 meeting in Chicago, airlines began to share information about their mistakes, and that openness has helped make flying one of the safest ways to travel. You rarely see that story in the press.

As Hans Rosling has written, there is no room for facts when our minds are full of fear.

Natural disasters get plenty of coverage, too. They are still devastating, but the death toll has shrunk. As countries get richer, they invest more in keeping people safe. The number of victims has fallen by half compared with a century ago, even though the world population has grown by five billion since 1900. That is a real success.

Sadly, the taxpayers who help fund that result, often through UN ReliefWeb, know very little about it. In Norway, only sixteen percent of people are aware of the progress. In Germany, the figure is just six percent. Remembering the progress keeps the fear instinct in check. Instead of obsessing over catastrophes, we should pay attention to the quiet problems like the lack of clean water and overfishing.

The number of nuclear warheads has dropped by about four times in less than forty years, from sixty-four thousand to fifteen thousand.

Data can also mislead. When the proportions are out of scale, we miss important nuance, and that is how the size instinct works. A good example is air pollution. Many people have heard that emerging economies like China and India pour out the most carbon dioxide. That is only part of the story. The other part is that the richest nations caused global warming in the first place, because their industrial emissions built up in the atmosphere over generations. China and India were on the first income level back then, and they had no role in that buildup.

Fourth-level countries still need to lower their own consumption. The richest one billion people are responsible for half of the fossil fuels burned. On a per-person basis, Indians burn about eight times less than Canadians. So fourth-level nations have to fix their own habits before pointing fingers at the emerging economies.

chapters.title: How can we guard ourselves against distorted facts?
chapters.text:
Everyone is vulnerable to the ten instincts we have walked through, even Hans Rosling himself. He spent his fourth year of medical school in Bangalore, India. In the very first class, the professor put up some X-ray images. Rosling spotted the issue right away, but he was too shy to speak up. To his surprise, almost every student in the room raised a hand and gave a thorough answer. He had nothing to add. For a moment, he figured he had wandered into a class of specialists, not fourth-year students. A few days later, he found out why. Indian textbooks were roughly three times as long as Swedish ones, and the students had them nearly memorized. It was his first lesson in the gap instinct behind the West-and-the-rest story.

So what tools can we use to keep our thinking honest?

Keep your knowledge fresh. Science moves quickly, and so do the numbers that come with it. Look closely at the way data is shown. A stretched vertical axis can make a small difference look huge, and a few changes can shrink that gap. Question words like most or minority, since they can mean almost anything. A majority could be fifty-one percent or ninety-seven percent. Try to find the exact number.

Take a closer look at the examples being used. A claim that ten percent of Brazilians earn forty-one percent of national income sounds like most people are in deep poverty. In fact, most Brazilians live at the second or third income level. Cross-check statistics with other sources. Rosling used this approach to confirm what he saw with his own eyes. Numbers alone cannot measure culture, so pair them with other kinds of evidence. Anyone with internet access can visit the Dollar Street website and see more than forty thousand photos and almost seventy-five hundred videos, showing four hundred forty-nine families from sixty-six countries.

As Rosling often said, the world cannot be understood without numbers, but the world cannot be understood with numbers alone.

Do not idealize the past. It is human to look back through rose-colored glasses, but that is self-deception. Rosling, for instance, was the first in his family to finish higher education. Notice slow progress. In 1900, only about three hundredths of a percent of land environments were protected. Today, the number is around fifteen percent.

chapters.title: Wrapping up
chapters.text:
The only cure for the ten instincts is factfulness. It gives you a helicopter view of the world, instead of a pile of fragments from the media. When you know the history behind a process and the context of a country, you can spot the trends behind the headlines. And those trends say the world is getting better.

That does not mean we should ignore the problems still in front of us. Extreme poverty, the dying seabed, and other big issues need our attention. Knowing about the progress gives us the hope we need to keep working on them.

So we should spread the truth that lifts people up. Rosling believed that awareness of the ten instincts should be part of every school program. It would help nurture the traits that matter most, like humility and curiosity. Admitting that you do not have all the answers is not a weakness. It is the start of a great journey. If we stay open to other beliefs and other experiences, we can pull our strengths together to face the worlds biggest challenges.

Fixing our distorted view is also a must for any serious work. In business, assuming that most of the world is poor hides billions of potential customers. In politics, it hides potential allies.

In the end, factfulness swaps an outdated map for a working GPS. It sharpens our choices and brings more joy to our days. Isnt it a relief to know that our effort is paying off?

Try this

Make it a habit to read about new findings in the social sciences, technology, and ecology each week. Visit the Dollar Street website to see how families at different income levels live in different countries. Set up a quiz with friends or coworkers using the materials at gapminder.org to see how much they know about the world.

Read it till the end?
